content:
People assume that Signals will definitely fix any memory leaks since there is no .subscribe(). This is an illusion which can be really harmful. The following shows how untracked side effects and the effect() loop can crash the browser.

6 min read
18 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Six months ago, when we migrated our enterprise application to Angular 22, we gleefully stripped away over 3,000 lines of old-school RxJS BehaviorSubject boilerplate from our analytics dashboard.
In place of the cumbersome takeUntilDestroyed() operators, we employed reactive Signals. The pull requests sailed through. The code felt more concise and elegant. We went into production, celebrated the upgrade, and then we forgot about it.
Three days after that, our alerting systems flagged a severity one incident. Our users on less powerful computers were suffering from critical browser lockups; their Chrome tabs were freezing with the infamous “Out of Memory” (OOM) error message.
What we failed to realize was that we have fallen prey to the most common architectural pitfall in today’s Angular world. Namely, the idea that since Signals don’t need manual unsubscription…
